steps.prompt: Which word is a verb form?

hints.gratia: 'gratia' is a noun meaning 'grace' — it is in the vocative/nominative
hints.plena: 'plena' is an adjective meaning 'full' — it modifies 'gratia'
hints.Dominus: 'Dominus' is a noun meaning 'the Lord' — it is the subject of the implied 'est'

hints.to have: 'to have' is 'habere' — 'Ave' comes from 'avere' (to hail/be well)
hints.to fly: 'to fly' is 'volare' or 'avolare' — 'Ave' comes from 'avere' (to hail)

hints.Indicative: 'Ave' is a greeting/command form — the indicative would be 'aves'
hints.Subjunctive: 'Ave' is a direct greeting — subjunctive would be 'aveas'
hints.Infinitive: Infinitive would be 'avere' — 'Ave' is the imperative singular

hints.habeo, habere, habui, habitum: These are the principal parts of 'habere' (to have)
hints.caveo, cavere, cavi, cautum: These are the principal parts of 'cavere' (to beware)
hints.faveo, favere, favi, fautum: These are the principal parts of 'favere' (to favor)

hints.Nominative: While 'gratia' could be nominative, here it is ablative of respect — 'full with/of grace'
hints.Genitive: Genitive of 'gratia' would be 'gratiae' — 'gratia' is the ablative form (ablative = nominative in 1st decl.)
hints.Vocative: Vocative would be 'gratia' (same form), but 'gratia' here is governed by 'plena' (full of)

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension nouns have genitive in -i — 'gratiae' ends in -ae
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension nouns have genitive in -is — 'gratiae' ends in -ae
hints.4th declension: 4th declension nouns have genitive in -ūs — 'gratiae' ends in -ae

steps.prompt: What does 'plena' mean and what does it govern?

hints.'full' — governs the genitive: While classical Latin 'plenus' can take genitive, in this Vulgate usage 'plena' takes the ablative 'gratia'
hints.'plain' — governs the dative: 'plena' means 'full', not 'plain' — and it governs the ablative here
hints.'full' — governs the accusative: 'plena' does not govern the accusative — it takes the ablative 'gratia'

hints.Genitive: Genitive of 'Dominus' would be 'Domini' — 'Dominus' is the nominative form
hints.Accusative: Accusative of 'Dominus' would be 'Dominum' — 'Dominus' is nominative, subject of implied 'est'
hints.Vocative: Vocative of 'Dominus' would be 'Domine' — 'Dominus' is the subject here

hints.'tec' + 'um' (your roof): 'tecum' is 'cum' (with) attached to 'te' (you) — a standard prepositional form
hints.'tec' + 'um' (your place): 'tecum' is the preposition 'cum' suffixed to the pronoun 'te'

translation: Hail, full of grace, the Lord is with you
